best_for: Fastest path to a PCI-compliant checkout — redirect to Stripe's own hosted payment page, minimal frontend work, built-in support for many payment methods
avoid_when: Needing full control over checkout UI/branding, or a fully embedded in-app checkout experience
tradeoffs: Stripe hosts the entire card-entry UI so you never touch raw card data (lowest PCI burden), at the cost of less visual control than a custom checkout

best_for: Product needing checkout to feel fully native/embedded in the app's own UI, while still avoiding raw card data touching your server
avoid_when: A team wanting the absolute minimum implementation effort — Checkout (EC001) is simpler to integrate
tradeoffs: Full UI control with Stripe handling the sensitive card input fields (via iframes) so raw card numbers still never hit your server, at the cost of more implementation and testing surface than hosted Checkout

best_for: Building a fully custom payment flow (e.g. a multi-step checkout, marketplace splitting) that needs Strong Customer Authentication (3D Secure) handled correctly
avoid_when: A standard checkout with no unusual flow requirements — Checkout or Elements already implement this correctly with less custom code
tradeoffs: Maximum flexibility for complex flows, but you're responsible for correctly handling every intent status (requires_action, requires_confirmation, etc.) — get this wrong and SCA-required payments silently fail

best_for: SaaS products with recurring revenue — Stripe manages subscription lifecycle, proration, invoicing, dunning (failed payment retry) natively
avoid_when: A pure one-time-purchase product with no recurring billing need
tradeoffs: Handles the genuinely hard parts of subscription billing (proration on plan changes, automatic retry on failed renewal, invoice generation) that are error-prone to build correctly from scratch

best_for: Products charging per-usage (API calls, compute time, seats that fluctuate) rather than a flat recurring fee
avoid_when: Simple flat-rate subscriptions — flat Stripe Billing (EC004) is simpler when usage isn't the pricing basis
tradeoffs: Report usage to Stripe via the API and let it handle proration/invoicing based on actual consumption, at the cost of needing reliable usage-reporting infrastructure on your end

option: Verify Every Webhook Signature Before Processing
best_for: Every Stripe webhook endpoint, without exception — this is the payments-specific application of SEC038's signature-verification rule
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — an unverified webhook endpoint can be triggered by anyone who discovers or guesses the URL, potentially spoofing a 'payment succeeded' event
tradeoffs: Verifying the Stripe-Signature header against the webhook secret is cheap and eliminates an entire class of spoofed-event risk — there is no legitimate reason to skip it

best_for: Every webhook handler — Stripe may deliver the same event more than once (on timeout/retry), so handlers must be safe to run twice for the same event id (BE046)
avoid_when: Never assume single delivery — build idempotent handling from the start rather than retrofitting after a double-processing incident (e.g. double-fulfilling an order)
tradeoffs: Use the event id as an idempotency key (store processed event ids, or make the underlying operation itself idempotent) so a duplicate delivery doesn't duplicate the side effect

option: Respond 2xx Quickly, Do Slow Work Asynchronously
best_for: Every webhook handler — acknowledge receipt fast (Stripe expects a response within a few seconds) and queue any slow follow-up work (sending emails, updating external systems) rather than doing it inline
avoid_when: A handler whose work is already trivially fast — no need to introduce a queue purely for ceremony if the whole handler reliably completes in milliseconds
tradeoffs: A slow webhook handler causes Stripe to consider the delivery failed and retry it, turning a slow-but-successful handler into a duplicate-delivery problem (compounding EC007's idempotency requirement) — this is BE051's async-job pattern applied to webhooks

option: Compute Charge Amounts Server-Side From the Order, Never Trust a Client-Supplied Total
best_for: Every payment flow — the amount charged is computed from the actual order/cart contents stored server-side, this is the payments-specific restatement of SEC040/SEC009
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — a tampered client-side price is the most direct way an attacker can under-pay if the server trusts a client-supplied amount
tradeoffs: Recomputing the charge amount server-side from trusted data is a small amount of extra code that closes off an entire exploit class, not an optional hardening step

option: Separate Test and Live API Keys, Never Mixed
best_for: Every environment — development/staging use Stripe test-mode keys (sk_test_/pk_test_) exclusively, production uses live keys (sk_live_/pk_live_) exclusively, matching the DO053 environment-separation principle
avoid_when: Never use a live key in a non-production environment even 'just to check something' — test-mode payment methods and real cards behave identically from the API's perspective
tradeoffs: Stripe's test mode fully simulates the payment flow (including specific test card numbers for success, decline, and 3DS-required scenarios) without ever touching real money — this discipline is what prevents an accidental real charge

option: Refund Logic Handles Partial Refunds and Updates Order State Consistently
best_for: Any product supporting refunds — the refund flow updates order/fulfillment state in your own database to match, not just issues the Stripe refund and stops there
avoid_when: A refund that only touches Stripe's records without updating your own order state — never leave this gap even for a 'simple' refund path
tradeoffs: Treat refund as a first-class state transition in your own order state machine (DB029), not a side-channel operation only visible in the Stripe dashboard, so your system and Stripe's records never drift apart

option: Dispute (Chargeback) Webhooks Trigger a Defined Internal Process
best_for: Any product accepting cards — chargeback/dispute webhook events trigger a defined process (notify a human, gather evidence, respond within Stripe's deadline)
avoid_when: Silently logging a dispute event with no active alert — never treat disputes as passive logging, the response deadline is real and enforced by Stripe
tradeoffs: Missing a dispute response deadline results in an automatic loss of the dispute regardless of its merits, so this needs an active alert wired to a human, not a log line nobody watches

option: Support Multiple Payment Methods via Stripe's Unified API, Not Just Cards
best_for: Products with an international or diverse customer base — Stripe's Payment Element/Checkout automatically offers regionally relevant methods (wallets, bank debits, buy-now-pay-later) without separate integration per method
avoid_when: A narrowly-scoped MVP where card-only is an acceptable initial launch simplification
tradeoffs: Higher conversion from offering locally-preferred payment methods, largely 'free' via Stripe's unified Payment Element rather than requiring separate integration work per method

best_for: Platforms paying out to multiple sellers/creators/contractors — Connect handles the split payment, KYC/onboarding of payees, and payout compliance
avoid_when: A single-recipient business (all revenue goes to one bank account) — plain Stripe Billing/Payments is simpler there
tradeoffs: Handles the genuinely hard parts of marketplace payments (regulatory KYC on payees, payout scheduling, tax form generation) that are extremely costly to build and maintain independently

option: Stripe Tax for Automatic Tax Calculation, Not Hand-Rolled Tax Logic
best_for: Any product selling across multiple tax jurisdictions — Stripe Tax calculates and can remit sales tax/VAT automatically based on customer location and product tax category
avoid_when: A single-jurisdiction business with a fixed, simple tax rate that doesn't vary — hand-rolled logic may be acceptable there, though still worth reassessing as the business expands
tradeoffs: Tax rules (rates, nexus thresholds, product categorization) change frequently and vary by jurisdiction — hand-rolled tax logic is a maintenance burden and compliance risk that a dedicated tax service is built to handle correctly

option: Never Expose the Stripe Secret Key Client-Side
best_for: Every integration — the secret key (sk_...) is used only in server-side code; only the publishable key (pk_...) is ever sent to the client — this is DO050's build-time-vs-runtime rule applied specifically to Stripe
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — the secret key can create charges, issue refunds, and access customer data; exposing it is a complete-compromise-level mistake
tradeoffs: There is no legitimate reason for the secret key to ever leave the server — treat any code path that would expose it client-side as a bug, not a tradeoff to weigh

option: Use Stripe's Idempotency-Key Header for Charge-Creating Requests
best_for: Any client-initiated request that creates a charge/payment intent — pass an idempotency key so a network retry (client timeout, double-click) doesn't create a duplicate charge
avoid_when: A read-only or non-mutating Stripe API call — idempotency keys matter specifically for requests that create a side effect
tradeoffs: This is BE058 applied to Stripe's own built-in idempotency mechanism — Stripe deduplicates requests with the same idempotency key within a 24-hour window automatically, at essentially no implementation cost
